author: @kreynolds1123
text:
Just observing, I see that pressure inside really does effect how much wind may blow in, and that adding height adds to the pressure inside, that in turn limits what percentage of air may enter inside rather than go around, and results in diminishing returns with additional height. But one may be place a turbine blade on both top and bottom helping to half the pressure buildup inside, and that the two  turbines may share the same axial shaft, driving a generator on the bottom, allowing for both easy access and a lower center of gravity. 

it would be fun to build one maybe 3m tall and 1m wide, and erect it 2m off the ground so that wind may blow underneath. 

--------
Reguarding efficency, It would be more appropriate to look at the cross section of wind rather than foot print, as one would with traditional horizontal and vertical axis wind turbines, i expectbit to be on par with or a little less than some common VAWT.  But reguardless off efficency, I'm excited about the potential for low cost, and easy of build.

author: @lajosruhzek2459
text:
I found the design of this lost technology wind turbine to be really cool, although not necessarily practical. I honestly don't think you need slats that move with the wind at all -- that loses energy to overcoming gravity constantly with no real payoff. As long as the slats are angled so that they point toward the turbine, it should be sufficient to produce the same or better effect. You're still capturing a greater wind surface area, and putting it in the right direction. 

The biggest question with making fixed slats though is what is the most appropriate angle range (which I think anywhere between 30-60 degrees from the horizontal would be fine), which would determine how far you want the slat to be from the turbine (too high versus too low and also inward versus outward), which is something your tower design doesn't really need to address, since the wind can only vent in one direction reliably, which is up.

Sadly, I can't say with any certainty my design would be better since I'm a complete novice with little to no understanding of the physics behind wind power.

text: I'm not sure about the claim that this design must be more efficient than a turbine on its own. author: @captainkeys1
text:
I made a small one of these once. The pressure and direction of the wind would push the flaps at the bottom down (the exit was at the top) maybe this won't happen at a larger scale. Assuming it does I have thought of two solutions.

1. Give it tall legs and allow air to vent from the bottom. This way the pressure will aid in pushing the flaps WITH gravity.
I'm not fond of this idea as much as it requires it to be tall and less safe as someone could go under and stick their hands into the blades. Also, back pressure from the ground may be an efficiency issue.

2. In the original design,(1:55) we see the flaps are flapping upward and are closed not by gravity but maybe wire with weights pulling the flaps closed. the weights would be just heavy enough to close but light enough that even a slight wind could blow them open and closed on the other side. This makes sense as the materials back them may have been heavier making flapping harder? The weights would fix not only the direction of flow but also allows you to use heavier/ cheaper materials.

replies.author: @captainkeys1
replies.text: After some thought: I don't think this is much of a problem on at larger scale. The benefits from 2 are likely too small at a larger scale to matter. Will play around with this anyways so things like steel can be used. (wood looks soo nice though!) the design in the video seems to be the best.

author: @makhoe1
text: Modern wind turbines make use of "apparent wind speed". The air isn't the only moving part of a modern wind turbine. The blades of a wind turbine move too. Apparent wind speed is the combination of wind speed and blade speed. Sailing vessels and ice yachts have used this principle to sail faster than the wind. Ice yachts have reportedly sailed ~8 or ~9 times wind speed. The aeromine cannot compete because apparent wind is a big deal. Vertical wind turbines cannot compete for the same reason.

author: @robertroberts5218
text: Just a few thoughts on improvement. At the base at the bottom if you could build an inverted funnel so that the air coming in has less turbulence and is more directed Up. And then at the top you would need to have a similar funneling. Your top board the hole is smaller than your octagon so that that distance from the edge of the Octagon to the edge of your swept area is going to be turbulent and wasted energy. So if you extended the tower up a little bit say a third do a half a meter to allow for a bit of a funneling from the edge of the Octagon to the swept area you would be more efficient directing that air through the turbine you'd have less turbulent air travel in the vertical shaft. I really like your mock-up it could go outside and go to work with just a little bit of all weather protection. If you were to make this for actual production I would suggest something less than plywood for the flaps maybe some diff plastic flaps it would be lighter. And and if it was to go into production, all the flat seats would need to be able to seal as the flap is in the closed position to reduce leakage. But overall I absolutely love this idea thank you so much for presenting harassment Darwin's work. You did a great job thank you again.

author: @AG-yb1lm
text:
You asked for this! 

Title : Tornado-Type Wind Turbine

Patent No US4070131A
Application : 1975
Inventor : James T. Yen
Assignee : Grumman Aerospace Corp

I had access to the corporate library back-then , extraordinary , all the engineering & prototyping was completed by Yen and Grumman.

They determined the diameter to height ratio. The temperature difference between the base and top of the silo to effectuate natural air flow. They built negative pressure louvers to allow air to be sucked in as the 'tornado' formed , etc..

You could use recycled heat at the base to cause cyclonic rotation in times of low outside ambient air flow. 

Have fun - would love to see you build it! 

Regards.

author: @TerryGilsenan
text:
the mass of the flaps is increasing the drag, and therefore reducing the available energy of the wind when it reaches the turbine. At low wind speeds I think this will have less ability to generate, until the wind speed (and therefore the air pressure on the windward side of the tower) can overcome the resistance to the air flow from the restriction caused by the need to raise the mass of the flaps.

At higher wind speeds, the swept area x air flow model will have a similar outcome to that of a force aspirated engine, in which the capacity is artificially increased and could overcome the additional load by increasing the effective swept area.

There will be a break-even point in the wind speed, below which the flap tower will be more load than benefit, and above which the load is less than the benefit it provides.

I do like the idea for its omnidirectional ability, and simplicity.

In a high wind location, this is a compact option.

author: @maxkennedy5073
text: Couple of thoughts use lightweight semi rigid flexible plastic and secure the flaps on the bottom not the top.  The plastic's springiness keeps them closed, the wind pushes them open and because they are secured on the bottom the top of the slat opens and the air is already being directed up so there would be less energy loss from the air having to bounce around inside the structure changing from moving down as it would with your hinged slats opening down.  If concerned with the plastic fatiguing where it flexes could use a real hinge with a tempered spring calibrated to open at a specific wind speed but that wouldn't be easy for DIY engineering. (spring clips somehow??)

author: @justinw1765
text:
A thought or two on possibly improving the design a bit.  That's a lot of flaps. If we use lighter weight materials, we probably could elongate the flaps so that there is less of them overall.  XPS foam or double corrugated cardboard reinforced with high quality epoxy (7k psi tensile strength or more) and S grade fiberglass cloth, makes some pretty light but tough and strong stuff. I don't like using epoxy much, but there is a company that tries to use as much bio content as they can while keeping high strengths and performance. They are called Entropy Resins.  I think their highest percentage of bio content epoxy is around 33% bio content. 

     Could one also put large scoops, billows, or the like on the outside so that the whole thing also rotates (the nice thing about this, is you could just use strong fabric for this, like sail or kite type fabrics)?  Connect another generator to that rotation?   I suppose this might interfere with the efficiency of the main turbine/generator though?

author: @edwardhigginson7307
text:
Hi Robert

I have been following your channel since August 
My search that found you was making a wind turbine for use camping, having purchased a Bluetti and 200w solar, I wasn’t impressed with the solar alone incidentally. With the Bluetti I have two 200w input

I really enjoy your enthusiasm it is infectious and your friendly knowledgeable style is inspirational
Most impressed.

This podcast reminded me of an idea i came across in the 80’s as i recall it was called a “uni temp” it appears now to have a whole new meaning, as i just googled it and there is nothing there
i sure I didn’t dream it. 
Anyhoo if you haven’t come across it before you might find it of interest

The basic idea is an internal column the height of a room, vented at the top and bottom 
With a differential thermostatic controlled fan at the top
Sucking in the warm air from the top of the room in and forcing it out of the bottom 
Really simple recycling 

That’s all there is to it

I hope it is of an interest to you

Again really enjoying your channel 

Thank you 

Edward

author: @AttilaBlade
text: Dear Robert, it was a nice video. I started to thinking about this tower. Basically I wanted to turns the top to the bottom. The panels should connecting with a rod or wire in column. If we put a weathercock onto the top and we connect the 8 wires onto a wobling plate that is turn around the tower freely as the wind blowing. It makes the tower more sensitive to the low speed wind. So basically the panel column could open automatically and the inner flow direction could be angled to the top turbine. Another idea: we can turn the air flow to a vortex with a simple modification. The panels are could open sidely. Then the flow will twist automatically and we can use a turbine in the whole tower structurw to generate electricity. The tower material is possible made from black sintered aluminium to increase the chimney effect. And one last thing, if we have a vortex then we have a lower pressure area in the middle section. So a tube could put in from the bottom with fresh air. This tube could generate electricity too from this suction energy. Sorry, if these are looks complicated, I just wanted to write what I thought about it! BLADE

Did you make those holes the exact size of the dowels?
If so, they may bind when they get wet and swell.

Just writing the above I thought of three ways to compensate for swelling dowels;
1) bees wax on the dowel and in the hole.
2) bore the hole 1 to 2 millimeters larger than the dowel.
3) go the expensive route and mount the dowels inside a bearing that is inserted into the frame upright.

author: @jasonmorello1374
text: I think, youwant to orient the device upside down from this, for a couple reasons. On some stilting probably. Less can fall in, such as rain and snow, a little loose bird netting will keep most birds out of it, either way, but low enough to not have deflection loss from the ground, and neighborhood cats will scare off birds that try to stay long. Second, this means you can keep the shaft of your generator at a lower and more accessible area. Might also consider an array of turbines. 6-8 20 cm turbines can stack some power generation, might be cheaper than one major one, and you can put it all thru one electric leveler/regulator set up, either to a or with a battery array. also covers partial failure, so if just one or so turbines fail or get jammed, you only have a production dip, not it all is gone.

author: @l0I0I0I0
text:
Worked with a professor from an ivy league college on Turbines and we had discussed the possibilities of a floating turbine using magnets or electromagnets, that could produce electricity.  He also said on another note, most any turbine can be 40-50% efficient (traditional turbines) but getting to that 97-99.5% range requires adjusting the blades to fit the situation including wind speed, friction, volume of fluid, etc.  Adjusting the blades and making them efficient can get expensive which is not what your after Rob.  However, 40% isn't bad so it's hard to go wrong.  I know, 40% of what.  😂
I would think one of your subscribers might be willing to print 3D printed turbines for three situations (for a fee) involving different wind speeds.  I would think it would be relatively easy to find a descent schematic for a relatively descent turbines in cad programs and print them out.  From here you can give directions on how to cast them in something stronger like aluminum.  Anyway, have fun!!!   Love this stuff!

text:
A few thoughts:
1.  The flaps could be made of fabric or film on a frame so as to be lower mass and take less of the wind force to swing them.
2.  Add megaphone shaped sail on top mounted on a bearing and sealed at the small end to the tower outlet.  The large end of the "topsail" would be pushed away from prevailing wind direction by any appreciable wind.  Thus you get a pressure drop behind the large end of the "topsail" giving not only a larger swept area but one at a higher elevation.  IF the image is not clear think of the sail as an inverted windsock with the small end anchored to a bearing around the tower air exit with a little internal framing to maintain cross section for good airflow.

author: @osmanfb1
text: Looks something like the 1970s invention  tornado type wind turbines. There were patents issued in the 70s and 80s but nothing commercial came out of them. These generate the pressure difference by directing the wind  tangentially into a cylindrical tower,causing it to rotate and generate low pressure at the center like yours the top is open and bottom is connected to the either horizontal or vertical  air turbine located below. Since the air turbine is ducted/shrouded it is more efficient than the unducted air turbines. Also was thought to work with even the low wind speeds. It would be possible to  base a design on one of the expired patents.

replies.author: @russellzauner
replies.text:
Not if you're using oscillation/deflection to harvest energy - put a stick the long way in your tower, tune the stick so it produces the vibrations you want, then you can use something like an LTC3588 (I've used these several times, they have worked well in many experiments) to simply hook up a piezo/strain transducer which will produce power directly without minimizing flow.   In fact, you want to encourage throughflow so your source stays as constant as possible.  🙂

copypasta for the LTC3588:
The LTC3588-1 integrates a low-loss full-wave bridge rectifier with a high efficiency buck converter to form a complete energy harvesting solution optimized for high output impedance energy sources such as piezoelectric, solar, or magnetic transducers. An ultralow quiescent current undervoltage lockout (UVLO) mode with a wide hysteresis window allows charge to accumulate on an input capacitor until the buck converter can efficiently transfer a portion of the stored charge to the output. In regulation, the LTC3588-1 enters a sleep state in which both input and output quiescent currents are minimal. The buck converter turns on and off as needed to maintain regulation.

Four output voltages, 1.8V, 2.5V, 3.3V and 3.6V, are pin selectable with up to 100mA of continuous output current; however, the output capacitor may be sized to service a higher output current burst. An input protective shunt set at 20V enables greater energy storage for a given amount of input capacitance.

author: @ericblenner-hassett3945
text:
The six sided wind milling from Arabia looks similar to that hard to find tech.  It was 6 walls, 2 with holes for air to go in and out with 1 wall between the ones with holes, the rest ' air tight '.  in the middle was 4 sheets on poles to catch the wind and rotate the main rotor that also had a milling stone on the top.  As to making a ' wing shape ' you just need a sheet of plastic that can be bent, glued and folded end to end with the middle still thick enough to ' round out ' instead of flatten.  You can see this effect with simple A4/A5 sheets if you fold it lengthwise ( harder to do in width wise ).
Edited to add the following:  The Airominer ( spelling? ) looks like 4 sheets of plastic, a pipe cut in half, builders board for the frame.  Glue the half pipe so the round is facing one of the open sides of the frame.  Glue in some of the sheet plastic, with a gap between the pipe, preferably folded to create the falling edge of a wing.  Then the other half of the pipe glued to the frame, facing the ' wing ' shape to help cause air flow to become more laminar and syphon up into the half pipe/sheet combination. The turbine will be hiding under the framework with the airflow up then out along the wing which is opposite the octagon example which pushes the air down.
